content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 15, 2007

Steven G. Churchwell
DLA Piper US, LLP
400 Capitol Mall, Suite 2400
Sacramento, CA 95814-4428

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-181

Dear Mr. Churchwell:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Mayor Steve Messina, regarding the "the public generally" exception to the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 (the "Act").¹ Because your request seeks general guidance, treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place.

We note that your original request for advice was made on behalf of Mayor Messina as well as council members Elizabeth Patterson, Alan Schwartzman, and Bill Whitney. However, for the purposes of this letter, we are limiting our advice to Mayor Messina's particular circumstances. As we discussed in a telephone conversation on January 25, 2007, the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing advice, and, ultimately, final factual determinations must be left up to the public officials. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Accordingly, we do not have sufficient facts to reach conclusions pertaining to Councilmember Patterson's, Councilmember Schwartzman's, or Councilmember Whitney's particular circumstances. Furthermore, we note that you have informed us, in a telephone conversation on January 31, 2007, that you are no longer seeking advice pertaining to Councilmember Whitney.

QUESTION

Providing that Mayor Messina is disqualified under the Act's conflict of interest provisions, does the "public generally" exception allow him to, nevertheless, make,

___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-06-181
Page No. 2

participate in making, or influence government decisions regarding the adoption of a
master plan for the Downtown Historic Conservation District (the "Downtown District")?

CONCLUSION

No, the public generally exception does not appear to apply to Mayor Messina's
circumstances.

FACTS

The City of Benicia is beginning to plan future commercial and mixed use
development in the Downtown District. In the upcoming months, the city council will be
adopting a master plan for the Downtown District. In addition to adopting the master
plan, the city council will also be making conforming changes to the current zoning
ordinance and to the general plan as needed.

Multiple governmental decisions pertaining to the adoption of the master plan are
anticipated including specific recommendations, implementation strategies, and "big
picture" goals and objectives. Generally, a "collective vision" will be developed to guide
the future implementation of the goals. The master plan will be used to help place
individual projects into the context of the overall vision. The master plan, itself, will
include an analysis of land uses and site utilization parameters, plus prescriptions for the
design of buildings. Other miscellaneous topics that may be considered include utilities
and infrastructure, transportation options and environmental conditions; a market demand
analysis and traffic studies; alternatives for development and circulation patterns in the
area; identification of development incentives; and development standards and design
guidelines for the study area.

You have provided the following facts relevant to the analysis of the "public
generally" exception:

• There are 591 business entities in Benicia; 224 (38 percent) of them are located in
the Downtown District or the surrounding 500 foot radius.

• There are 11,162 property owners in Benicia; 1,362 (12.2 percent) of these are
located within the Downtown District or within 500 feet of its boundary.

• The population of Benicia is 27,319, of which 3,151 people (11.5 percent) live
downtown.³

³ While you indicated in your request for advice that 1,189 people live downtown, in a telephone
conversation on October 10, 2006, you corrected this figure and provided that 3,151 people live downtown.

File No. I-06-181
Page No. 3

Mayor Messina owns two properties inside or within 500 feet of the Downtown
District including a vacant lot and a rental property. Mayor Messina also owns an ice
cream/sandwich shop in the Downtown District. As an additional note, Mayor Messina's
economic interests are more thoroughly detailed in our previous letter issued to you,
Churchwell Advice Letter, No. A-06-130.

ANALYSIS

The "Public Generally" Exception

Even if a public official determines that his or her economic interests will
experience a material financial effect as a result of the governmental decision before the
official, he or she may still participate under the "public generally" exception if the
material financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic
interests is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103;
regulation 18707.)

Regulation 18707 (b) sets forth a four-step process to determine "if the effect of a
decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally:"

"(1) Step One: Identify each specific person or real property (economic
interest) that is materially affected by the governmental decision.

"(2) Step Two: For each person or real property identified in Step One,
determine the applicable 'significant segment' rule according to the
provisions of [regulation 18707.1(b)].

"(3) Step Three: Determine if the significant segment is affected by the
governmental decision as set forth in the applicable 'significant segment'
rule. If the answer is 'no,' then the analysis ends because the first prong
of a two-part test set forth in [regulation 18707.1(b)] is not met, and the
public official cannot participate in the governmental decision. If the
answer is 'yes,' proceed to Step Four.

"(4) Step Four: Following the provisions of [regulation 18707.1(b)(2)],
determine if the person or real property identified in Step One is affected
by the governmental decision in 'substantially the same manner' as other
persons or real property in the applicable significant segment. If the
answer is 'yes' as to each person or real property identified in Step One,
then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally and the public official may participate in the decision. If
the answer is 'no' as to any person or real property identified in Step One,
the public official may not participate in the governmental decision unless
one of the special rules set forth in [regulations 18707.2 through 18707.9]

File No. I-06-181
Page No. 4

applies to each person or real property triggering the conflict of interest."
(Regulation 18707(b)(1)-(4).)

Step One: Mayor Messina's potential conflict of interest was addressed in our
previous advice letter to you, the Churchwell Letter, supra. For purposes of this letter,
you have asked us to presume that the governmental decisions will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on the various economic interests of Mayor Messina.
Accordingly, we presume that the governmental decisions will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on Mayor Messina's economic interests identified
herein and discussed below.

Step Two:

Individual Source of Income (Tenants of Rental Property and/or Customers of Ice
Cream/Sandwich Shop) – The applicable "significant segment" for decisions that affect
the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of a public official or a member of his
or her immediate family, or that affect an individual who is a source of income or a
source of gifts to a public official, under regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(A), is "[t]en percent or
more of the population in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the
official represents" or "5,000 individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction."

Real Property (Vacant Lot and Rental Property) – The applicable "significant
segment" for decisions that affect an interest in real property, under regulation
18707.1(b)(1)(B)(i), is "[t]en percent or more of all property owners or all residential
property owners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official
represents" or "5,000 property owners or residential property owners in the jurisdiction of
the official's agency."

Business Entities (Rental Business and Ice Cream/Sandwich Shop) – The
applicable "significant segment" for decisions that affect an interest in a business entity,
under regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(C), is "either 2,000 or twenty-five percent of all business
entities in the jurisdiction or the district the official represents, so long as the effect is on
persons composed of more than a single industry, trade, or profession."

Step Three:

Individual Source of Income (Tenants of rental property and/or Customers of Ice
Cream/Sandwich Shop) – You have provided that 3,151 (11.5 percent) of Benicia's
27,319 residents live downtown. If a specific governmental decision pertaining to the
Downtown District will affect all residents living downtown, the minimal threshold of ten
percent of the population, as provided in regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(A), has been met.⁴

⁴ While you have provided that 3,151 (11.5 percent) of the City of Benicia's 27,319 residents live
downtown, that 1,362 (12.2 percent) of the 11,162 property owners within the city are located in or within
500 feet of the Downtown District, and that 224 (38 percent) of the city's 591 business entities are located

File No. I-06-181
Page No. 5


Real Property (Vacant Lot and Rental Property) – You have provided that 1,362 feet of the 11,162 property owners within the city are located in or within 500 feet of the Downtown District. If a specific governmental decision pertaining to the Downtown District will affect all 1,362 property owners in or within 500 feet of the Downtown District, the minimal threshold of ten percent of the property owners, as provided in regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(B)(i), has been met.

Business Entities (Rental Business and Ice Cream/Sandwich Shop) – You have provided that 224 (38 percent) of the city's 591 business entities are located in or within 500 feet of the Downtown District. If a specific governmental decision pertaining to the Downtown District will affect all 224 business entities in or within 500 feet of the Downtown District, the minimal threshold of twenty-five percent of the business entities, as provided in regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(C), has been met.

Step Four: The final step requires Mayor Messina to determine the financial effects on his respective economic interests and measure these effects against the financial effects on the public generally. What becomes critical for Mayor Messina is the identification that the minimum threshold of the significant segment identified is affected in substantially the same manner.

Many factors play a role in determining whether or not economic interests will be financially affected in a manner substantially the same as the significant segment identified including, but not limited to: the proximity of an individual, property, or business to a specific project or property subject to the government decision; location of a property or business; impact on an individual, property, or business from noise or traffic; impact on a business or property due to development potential; and the size of a property or business. (See Berger Advice Letter, No. A-05-054; also see regulation 18707.1(b)(2)(A).)

Real Property (Vacant Lot and Rental Property) – Mayor Messina has multiple economic interests in real property within the Downtown District. In the Shirey Advice Letter, No. A-06-132, we stated that "given the fact that there are two properties owned by the Commissioner, it is unlikely that the dollar value impact on these two properties will be substantially the same as on property owners' homes where they only own one home." Similarly, because Mayor Messina owns both a rental property and a vacant lot, it is unlikely that other property owners within the downtown district will be financially affected in substantially the same manner as Mayor Messina.

_______________________________________________________________________________
in or within 500 feet of the Downtown District, we must note that the mere fact that the residents, property owners, and business entities are located in or within 500 feet of the Downtown District does not conclusively establish that the residents, property owners, or business entities will be affected by the governmental decisions pertaining to the downtown district.

File No. I-06-181
Page No. 6

Moreover, one of Mayor Messina's real property interests is a vacant lot. The fact
that Mayor Messina's lot is vacant suggests that any financial effect on the lot's
developmental potential will be greater than the effect on other previously developed lots.
Considering this fact, again, it is unlikely that other property owners within the
downtown district will be financially affected in substantially the same manner.

Individual Source of Income (Tenant of rental property and/or Customers of Ice
Cream/Sandwich Shop) and Business Entities (Rental Business and Ice Cream/Sandwich
Shop) – In terms of Mayor Messina's economic interests in the individual sources of
income and business entities, we again have not been provided with sufficient facts to
determine whether these interests will be affected in substantially the same manner as the
applicable significant segment. However, as we have reached a conclusion that the
public generally exception does not appear to apply to Mayor Messina's circumstances
because of his multiple property interests, it is unnecessary for us to reach any further
conclusions pertaining to Mayor Messina's other economic interests.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

BGLjl
